contest.title: 2024-2025 ICPC, NERC, Northern Eurasia Finals (Unrated, Online Mirror, ICPC Rules, Teams Preferred)

sources.notes: Canonical problem identity. Statement and constraints were cross-checked against the official contest statement PDF.
Official statement used to verify the pointer restrictions, adversarial guarantee, special outputs, and n,m <= 4e5 constraints.
Official tutorial. Used for the vertex-type characterization, immediate k-vertices, shortest-path retroanalysis, and complexity.
Luogu mirror used to cross-check the Chinese statement and the exact meaning of following a pointer versus wandering.
Independent solution. Cross-checked the equivalent 0/1-edge safety-distance interpretation and the two-phase reverse propagation.
Inspected jury source runs/jury-I-4-incompetent-driver_mi_cpp.cpp. It implements reverse Dijkstra, tight-edge outdegree counters, and alternating BFS/retrograde queues.
Independently checked the min-deviations-to-failure interpretation, off-by-one levels, unreachable entrance, infinite-safety, loops, parallel edges, and 64-bit distances.

reference_candidates.notes: The solution page was not publicly readable during this review, so it was not used as evidence.

annotation.summary: Choose one outgoing edge lying on a shortest path to vertex n as the pointer at every vertex that can reach n. An adversarial orc may ignore pointers at most d times and choose arbitrary outgoing edges. Find the largest d for which some pointer assignment guarantees reaching n from vertex 1.
annotation.constraints: 2 <= n <= 4e5, 0 <= m <= 4e5, positive edge weights up to 1e6; directed loops and parallel edges are allowed. Distances require 64-bit integers.
annotation.core_idea: Let type[u] be the maximum number of deviations that can always be survived from u under an optimal pointer assignment. Vertices unable to reach n have type -1, while vertices that remain unclassified have infinite type. First run Dijkstra from n on the reversed graph. Edges satisfying dist[u] = w + dist[v] are the legal pointer edges; positive weights make them a DAG. Starting from all type -1 vertices, process types in increasing order. If any outgoing edge u->v reaches a vertex of type k-1, then u becomes an immediate candidate of type k because one deviation can enter that layer. Within the same layer, propagate backward through legal pointer edges: once every legal pointer edge of u leads to an already absorbed vertex, no pointer choice can avoid that layer, so u receives the same type. Tight outgoing-edge counters and two queues implement these existential and universal transitions in linear total time after Dijkstra. Vertex 1's type is the answer; an unclassified vertex is reported as n.
annotation.complexity: O((n + m) log n) time and O(n + m) memory.
tricks: Replace randomness by an adversary because delivery must be guaranteed for every possible sequence of choices.
Interpret type[u] + 1 as the minimum number of non-pointer edges needed to reach an unreachable vertex under an optimal pointer assignment.
Legal pointer edges strictly decrease shortest-path distance and therefore form a DAG.
Alternate arbitrary-edge predecessor expansion, which increases the type by one, with all-tight-successors retrograde closure, which preserves the type.
Ignore outgoing edges of Orthanc because reaching vertex n terminates the process.
Use n as the finite representation of infinity: every finite type consumes at least one newly classified vertex.

solution_variants.name: dijkstra-retrograde-leveling
solution_variants.summary: Run reverse Dijkstra, count each vertex's tight outgoing edges, initialize unreachable vertices with type -1, and maintain two work queues. Processing a fixed vertex through all incoming edges proposes predecessor type +1. Processing the retrograde queue decrements counters only along tight incoming edges; when a predecessor has no remaining unabsorbed tight successor, assign it the current type. Every edge participates only constantly many times.
solution_variants.complexity: O((n + m) log n) time and O(n + m) memory.

tags.evidence: Distances to Orthanc determine reachability and exactly which outgoing edges may be selected as pointers; reverse Dijkstra is indispensable at n,m <= 4e5.
The decisive post-Dijkstra algorithm propagates terminal failure levels backward, combining an existential arbitrary-edge transition with an all-tight-successors counter transition.
tags.description: Propagate solved terminal states or outcome levels backward through a directed state graph, using predecessor worklists and all-successor counters to resolve adversarial, game, or safety states. Use when reverse attractor/outcome propagation is central, not for an ordinary reverse BFS.
tags.created_reason: Existing graph/bfs and graph/dag tags do not identify CF2052I's essential AND/OR reverse propagation: one arbitrary bad edge raises the level, while every legal pointer successor must be absorbed before a vertex joins the same level.

tags.evidence: All legal pointer edges strictly decrease dist because weights are positive, so their acyclic structure makes the all-successor retrograde closure well-founded.
Although wandering is described as random, the required guarantee forces the solution to handle every possible outgoing-edge choice adversarially.
